issues.title: [Android] Keyboard not showing at startup on TextField has `autofocus: true`
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

1. Execute `flutter run` on the code sample <!-- (see "Code sample" section below) --> on Android device

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->

Expected: Keyboard should show

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->

Expected: Keyboard not showing

To my understanding setting TextField's autofocus to true should open the keyboard automatically.
I have not found any way to open the keyboard automatically at startup.

<details>
<summary>Code sample</summary>

```import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: const MyHomePage(),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget {
  const MyHomePage({super.key});
  @override
  State<MyHomePage> createState() => _MyHomePageState();
}

class _MyHomePageState extends State<MyHomePage> {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(body: Column(children: [
        Expanded(child: Center(child: Container(
          width: 200, height: 40,
          child: const TextField(autofocus: true,)
        )))
    ]));
  }
}
```

</details>

issues.title: TextField textbaseline ignores padding with outline border
comments:
The bug is reproducible using the code sample provided above. 

<details>
<summary>recording</summary>

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/88313112/231986193-2ae8b976-9853-4528-b270-f843c1f038cb.mov
</details>

<details>
<summary>updated code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MainApp());
}

class MainApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MainApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    const contentPadding = EdgeInsets.fromLTRB(10, 50, 10, 10);

    return MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        body: Center(
          child: Padding(
            padding: const EdgeInsets.all(8.0),
            child: Column(
              mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
              children: const [
                Text('TextField With OutlinedInputBorder'),
                TextField(
                  decoration: InputDecoration(
                    border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                    contentPadding: contentPadding,
                  ),
                  textAlignVertical: TextAlignVertical.top,
                ),
                Text('TextField With OutlinedInputBorder & isCollapsed: true'),
                TextField(
                  decoration: InputDecoration(
                    border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                    isCollapsed: true,
                    contentPadding: contentPadding,
                  ),
                  textAlignVertical: TextAlignVertical.top,
                ),
                Text('TextField With UnderlineInputBorder'),
                TextField(
                  decoration: InputDecoration(
                    border: UnderlineInputBorder(),
                    contentPadding: contentPadding,
                  ),
                  textAlignVertical: TextAlignVertical.top,
                ),
              ],
            ),
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

issues.title: [web] Typing compound characters generates unexpected deltas on Safari
comments:
In super_editor, inserting a compound character like `ã` by typing `~`+ `a` on a Brazilian keyboard isn't working on Safari.

Typing `~` first generates the following delta: 

```dart
  TextEditingDeltaInsertion(
      oldText: 'type here:',
      textInserted: '~',
      insertionOffset: 10,
      selection: TextSelection(baseOffset: 10, extentOffset: 11),
      composing: TextRange(start: 10, end: 11),
    )
```

The selection in this delta is wrong, as it should be a collapsed selection. Then, a non-text delta is reported setting the correct selection, in a separate call to `updateEditingValueWithDeltas`.

The issue is that, after we handle the insertion delta, we end up with a different editing value from what the IME thinks it is. On the IME it has an expanded selection and on the editor it has a collapsed selection. As we detect that the value differs, we send back our editing value to the IME by calling `setEditingState`. This prevents the non-text delta from being reported.

Calling `setEditingState` between the two `updateEditingValueWithDeltas` causes the text to be inserted as `~ã` instead of `ã`.

This might be related to https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/129061.


@Renzo-Olivares @justinmc
CC @justinmc @Renzo-Olivares

@Renzo-Olivares recently added some improvements to the text editing delta system - do you have any thoughts on this one?
@htoor3 I can take a look at this one if that's okay with you. I've fixed a similar issue on other platforms.
Thank you @Renzo-Olivares! Sounds good to me.

Hi @angelosilvestre, thanks for reporting this issue. I'm not sure I follow completely why receiving a delta that collapses the selection after the initial delta causes the IME and the editor to become out of sync. Is there a modification being made by the editor to the editing state between the deltas `TextEditingDeltaInsertion('~')`, and `TextEditingDeltaNonTextUpdate(TextSelection.collapsed)`?

I was also able to observe the reported behavior using `TextField` on Safari, so it is not specific to deltas.

Hi @Renzo-Olivares. Thanks for looking into this.

> I'm not sure I follow completely why receiving a delta that collapses the selection after the initial delta causes the IME and the editor to become out of sync

The issue isn't the non-text delta, is that between the two deltas, we detect that our selection is different from the IME selection. We receive the first delta:

```dart
  TextEditingDeltaInsertion(
      oldText: 'type here:',
      textInserted: '~',
      insertionOffset: 10,
      selection: TextSelection(baseOffset: 10, extentOffset: 11),
      composing: TextRange(start: 10, end: 11),
    )
```

When we perform the insertion, super_editor already ends up with a collapsed selection. Therefore, we send our value with the collapsed to the IME. After pressing `a`, we receive an `ã` insertion instead of replacing `~`with `ã`.

@angelosilvestre Okay to make sure I understand, you're saying `super_editor` is ignoring the selection changes from the `TextEditingDeltaInsertion` being received from the engine and solely using the `insertionOffset` and `textInserted` to determine the new editing state after the delta? If so I can look further into why Safari/ webkit sets an uncollapsed selection here. What might be possible is batching the `TextEditingDeltaInsertion` with the `TextEditingDeltaNonTextUpdate` that fixes the selection, then that delta batch would have to be processed together.

@Renzo-Olivares Yes, an insertion delta might trigger additional reactions which could change the document, making the IME current selection undesirable, or even invalid.

For example, typing `## ` could convert the current paragraph into a header, removing the `## `. The IME selection (at offset 3) won't be a valid selection, as the paragraph is now an empty header.

This issue is assigned to @Renzo-Olivares but has had no recent status updates. Please consider unassigning this issue if it is not going to be addressed in the near future. This allows people to have a clearer picture of what work is actually planned. Thanks!
This issue was assigned to @Renzo-Olivares but has had no status updates in a long time. To remove any ambiguity about whether the issue is being worked on, the assignee was removed.

issues.title: Layout jumps/stutters on first keyboard open after rotation
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

On a physical Android device (Samsung S9):
 
1. Launch the code sample (either **Start Debugging** or `flutter run --release`).
2. Rotate device to landscape.
3. Tap the text field to open keyboard, then back button to close the keyboard.
4. Rotate back to portrait.
5. Tap text field again → body jumps up for a very brief time before settling down at correct position.

The same issue occurs by reversing the orientation order (open keyboard in Portrait, close it, change orientation to Landscape, open keyboard again), though in that case it doesn't jump up, but stays hidden down.

It seems like the wrong orientation information is applied for a few frames before everything jumps into place.

### Expected results

Body (in this case the lone TextField) should animate smoothly into the correct vertical location when the virtual keyboard opens

### Actual results

Huge vertical overshoot, then jumps into the correct place (when Landscape → portrait)

or

Body remain hidden behind keyboard then appears in view (when Portrait → Landscape)

### Code sample

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MainApp());
}

class MainApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MainApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        body: Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.end,
          children: [
            Padding(
              padding: const EdgeInsets.all(50.0),
              child: TextField(
                decoration: InputDecoration(
                  hintText: 'Text',
                  enabledBorder: OutlineInputBorder(borderSide: BorderSide(color: Colors.black, width: 1.0)),
                  focusedBorder: OutlineInputBorder(borderSide: BorderSide(color: Colors.black, width: 1.0)),
                ),
              ),
            ),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

issues.title: [iOS] text field loses focus after Look Up text selection
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

App is launched on iOS 17.2 (reproduces both on a simulator and on a real device)

1. Select text in Text Field
2. Press Look up
3. Close the Look up dialog

### Expected results

Keyboard hides or text field returns its focus

### Actual results

Keyboard persists on screen, but the text field does not retain focus and keyboard does not hide via `unfocus()` (see video)

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
```

issues.title: Using WidgetTester.enterText on a disabled TextFormField breaks future calls
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill out the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- You must include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Use `enterText` on a `TextFormField`. See that it works.
2. Disable and renable the form and see that `enterText` continues to work.
3. This time, disable the field and use `enterText`. See that does not change the text value, as expected.
4. Re-enable the field and see that `enterText` doesn't work despite being enabled again.

`enterText` only breaks after having used it on a disabled field and then re-enabling it.

Here is an example of a failing test when the commented line is uncommented:

```
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
import 'package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart';

class TestFieldDisable extends StatefulWidget {
    @override
    State<StatefulWidget> createState() => TestFieldDisableState();
}

class TestFieldDisableState extends State<TestFieldDisable> {

    bool _enabled = true;

    set enabled(bool to) => setState(() {
        _enabled = to;
    });

    @override
    Widget build(BuildContext context) => TextFormField(enabled: _enabled);

}

void main() {

    testWidgets("TextFormField should accept text when renabled", (tester) async {

        await tester.pumpWidget(
            MaterialApp(home: Scaffold(body: TestFieldDisable()))
        );

        final disablerState = tester.state<TestFieldDisableState>(
            find.byType(TestFieldDisable)
        );

        final fieldFinder = find.byType(TextFormField);
        final fieldState = tester.state<FormFieldState>(fieldFinder);

        await tester.enterText(fieldFinder, "one");
        await tester.pump();
        expect(fieldState.value, "one");

        disablerState.enabled = false;
        await tester.pump();

        // await tester.enterText(fieldFinder, "two"); // Uncomment to break
        await tester.pump();
        expect(fieldState.value, "one");

        disablerState.enabled = true;
        await tester.pump();

        await tester.enterText(fieldFinder, "two");
        await tester.pump();
        expect(fieldState.value, "two");

    });

}
```

**Expected results:**

`enterText` should continue to work when the field is enabled again.

**Actual results:**

The final `expect` fails due to remaining as "one" instead of "two".

<details>
  <summary>Logs</summary>

<!-- Finally, paste the output of running `flutter doctor -v` here. -->

```
[✓] Flutter (Channel stable, 2.5.1, on Debian GNU/Linux 10 (buster) 4.19.0-17-amd64, locale en_GB.UTF-8)
    • Flutter version 2.5.1 at...
    • Upstream repository https://github.com/flutter/flutter.git
    • Framework revision ffb2ecea52 (2 days ago), 2021-09-17 15:26:33 -0400
    • Engine revision b3af521a05
    • Dart version 2.14.2
```

</details>

issues.title: [MacOS] LogicalKeyboard involving shift key throws error: Physical key for LogicalKeyboardKey not found in macos physical key map
issues.body:
When I try to press the exclamation logical keyboard key using `WidgetTester`, I get the following error:

```
Error: 'package:flutter_test/src/event_simulation.dart': Failed assertion: line 243 pos 12: 'result != null': Physical key for LogicalKeyboardKey#00021(keyId: "0x00000021", keyLabel: "!", debugName: "Exclamation") not found in macos physical key map
```

Flutter version:

@dkwingsmt Is this related to your keyboard work?

This is behaving as designed, because there is no "!" key on the keyboard, but only Shift-Digit1. You should dispatch a "ShiftLeft down, Digit1 down, Digit1 up, ShiftLeft up".

...unless you're simulating a French AZERTY keyboard. Yes, in that case the Digit1 key gives an exclamation mark naturally. But then you should use
```dart
tester.sendKeyDownEvent(LogicalKeyboardKey.exclamation, platform: "macos", physicalKey: PhysicalKeyboardKey.digit1);
```

@dkwingsmt if this is intended behavior, then how are developers expected to input arbitrary characters when testing keyboard input?

Unless I'm missing some other set of Flutter test APIs, the only thing that I can think of is that every app will need to maintain their own mapping that connects every logical key to its physical key + shift status, per keyboard type, and then use that to construct the "intended" key event.

If that's the intended behavior, it creates identical work for every app, forces app developers to educate themselves on the various keyboard configurations, and it forces them to figure out how the test system utilizes key events. I think that's a pretty strong argument that Flutter should not expect developers to interact with keyboards in this manner within their tests. I think that this is probably disincentivizing testing, rather than promoting it.

Here's a test tool in Super Editor where we need to allow our users to enter arbitrary text into the field:
https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/blob/main/super_editor/lib/src/infrastructure/super_textfield/_test_tools.dart#L44

That method will blow up if the user tries to type any shift-based character.

It would be nice if Flutter didn't require each developer to know every character that requires shift to be pressed. However, I went ahead and wrote all of them down in the aforementioned tool.

What I didn't map out was the relationship between every logical key and its associated shift-based value. That's another mapping that it sure would be nice if Flutter handled for us.

Can Flutter introduce APIs that represent the way that app developers think about keyboard input, rather than APIs that strictly adhere to internal Flutter details? I think that would be much more valuable when we're talking about top-level testing APIs.

---

On a related node, is there a reason that the [`sendKeyDownEvent()`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/flutter_test/WidgetController/sendKeyDownEvent.html) method supports a `character` argument, but [`sendKeyEvent()`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/flutter_test/WidgetController/sendKeyEvent.html) doesn't?

This API difference requires explicit "down" and "up" events when specifying a `character`, even though those two events constitute a single "key event".

Update: I just merged in a contribution from someone else that went ahead and added that shift mapping for a US English keyboard. Everything in my previous response remains the same, except the Super Editor code now works for US English use-cases.

I think you've mixed "having this feature in production" with "testing this feature".

## Use keyboard events

For how to develop a keyboard feature that deals with characters: The native macOS API provides two kinds of information regarding the character: `characters`, which would be "@" in your example, and `charactersIgnoringModifiers`, which would be "2" in your example. Flutter converts the former to `KeyEvent.character`, and the latter (roughly) to `KeyEvent.logicalKey`. (I'm not that sure with the legacy `RawKeyboard` API but I would assume it's similar; let me know if not.) So you if you want the user to input arbitrary text, append the `KeyEvent.character` value to your state. Or maybe you should checkout the text input API, which also handles IME?

## Test keybaord events

First off, instead of `WidgetController.sendKeyEvent` (and siblings), you might want to check out `simulateKeyDownEvent` (and siblings.) They allow customizing `physicalKey` and `character`. Yes, the `WidgetController.sendKeyEvent` methods are just wraps over them, and it's an oversight that these APIs are not exposed. We should fix it.

Now, if that solves your problem, great. If not, I'll continue explaining.

The mapping between physical keys, logical keys, and characters, is completely arbitrary. It's entirely possible to have a `LogicalKeyboardKey("@")`. Sure, create it on your own, simulate it in tests, and very likely it will be sent by some embedding.

The problem is, it will never be sent by a macOS embedding. Simulating a keyboard event (for the `RawKeyboard` API) requires converting your key event back to a macOS native key event, senting it to `RawKeyboard`, which then converts it again to `RawKeyEvent` API. But if macOS never sends a `LogicalKeyboardKey("@")` value, what native event can it even be converted to?

(This will be easier to understand once we migrate everything to the `HardwareKeyboard` API, whose framework part knows nothing about the native information.)

Furthermore, what exactly do you want to test? The user can input "@" via a variety of ways:
- macOS, US keyboard, Shift-2.
- macOS, ASERTY keyboard, 2.
- Windows, US keyboard, Shift-2.
- macOS, custom keyboard layout, CapsLock (for random reasons.)

Do you really want to test out all the infinite cases? Or do you only care about processing key events where `KeyEvent.character` equals to `"@"`, no matter what their `physicalKey` and `logicalKey` are?

That's why the key event simulation API allows customizing all 3 fields: `logicalKey`, `physicalKey`, and `character`. The latter 2 fields are derived by default for convenience, but you shouldn't think that the default behavior is all that Flutter assumes.

@dkwingsmt to make sure that I'm not misunderstanding you, can you write an example `WidgetTester` invocation that sends the "@" character in the manner that you think a Flutter developer should use in their tests?

@dkwingsmt I think that would technically be work-able. 

I think it's worth considering whether Flutter should provide some common keyboard configurations so that app developers don't need to manually connect the "@" character to the "2" key. That level of control might be good at the ground level, but I think it's going to feel out of place in the common case.

In fact, I think it might make sense for Flutter to support the actual use-case that I'm dealing with, which is pressing keys that correspond to arbitrary characters. If that's not something that Flutter wants to offer, then it would be helpful to know where Flutter sees the boundary between the built-in test capabilities vs individual app developer test responsibilities.

One last point, the use of the word "simulate" in these calls makes them seem fundamentally different from existing `WidgetTester` calls, e.g., "sendKeyEvent". Are these calls fundamentally different? If so, please be sure to clearly describe those differences in the API docs for any new methods.

Heads up: I'm changing the behavior regarding Shift'ed keys and logical keys on macOS, Linux, and Web. The new behavior aligns with that's already on Windows, and should be more predictable and intuitive, and partly align with what you said in this issue. Some examples are:

| When pressing physical keys...| on layout | Previous logical keys | New logical keys |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | US | `digit1` | `digit1` (unchanged) |
| Shift-1 | US | `shiftLeft + exclamation` | `shiftLeft + digit1` |
| 1 | French | `ampersand` | `digit1` | 
| Shift-1 | French | `shiftLeft + digit1` | `shiftLeft + digit1` (unchanged) |
| a | Russian | `ф` | `keyA` |

This means that you will be able to write test for `shiftLeft + digit1` and expect it to work.

This will probably be a breaking change, but I'm still evaluating options.

For more information and tracking, see https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/100456

Japanese qwerty and Chinese qwerty usually keep a buffer for the input in english until the user escapes confirming the input. Korean has doesn't buffer english input but rather builds the characters in place from the korean alphabet, if the next character input can be combined with the built syllable, it will combine them. A character in korean is complete when the next input korean character cannot be combined or the user escapes manually. 

ㄱ,ㅏ,ㅂ are there different component characters in the korean alphabet. Combine to make 갑 (gap) i can add a ㅅ here to make 값. If I add another vowel, the input would actually separate the ㅂ to 가벼 because a vowel in korean must always follow a consonant.

issues.title: Flutter 3.19.4 | Android Specific | `enableSuggestions = false` on `TextField` causing emoji button to disappear.
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce Use a `TextField` and set `enableSuggestions` to `false`. ``` TextField(enableSuggestions: false) ``` This will result in emoji button, which opens emoji keyboard, to disappear. Not sure if Emojis have anything to do with suggestions. #### Note: Tried similar code on my older Flutter 3.13.4 version, and `enableSuggestions` have no impact. It doesn't even remove suggestions box. Seems like a bug back then and the fix must have also caused this side effect. ### Expected results Emoji button should not disappear when suggestions are turned off `enableSuggestions: true` | `enableSuggestions: false` :-------------------------:|:-------------------------: | ### Actual results `enableSuggestions: true` | `enableSuggestions: false` :-------------------------:|:-------------------------: | ### Code sample
Code sample ```dart import 'package:flutter/material.dart'; void main() { runApp(const MyApp()); } class MyApp extends StatelessWidget { const MyApp({super.key}); @override Widget build(BuildContext context) { return MaterialApp( title: 'Flutter Demo', theme: ThemeData( colorScheme: ColorScheme.fromSeed(seedColor: Colors.deepPurple), useMaterial3: true, ), home: Scaffold( appBar: AppBar( backgroundColor: Colors.red, title: Text('O_o'), ), body: const Center(child: TextField(enableSuggestions: false)), )); } } ```

issues.title: Broken text selection when selecting backwards from `TextSpan` into `WidgetSpan` within `SelectionArea`
comments: Note: This issue is predominantly seen when rendering Markdown within Flutter apps, since Markdown parsing results in a mixed tree of WidgetSpan and TextSpan's. Happy to share a reproducible sample with this use case if needed.

Reproducible on latest stable and the master channel.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4f92c461-842e-447c-8681-823340e329d1


<details>
<summary>code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Flutter Demo',
      theme: ThemeData(
        colorScheme: ColorScheme.fromSeed(seedColor: Colors.deepPurple),
      ),
      home: const MyHomePage(title: 'Flutter Demo Home Page'),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyHomePage({super.key, required this.title});

  final String title;

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        backgroundColor: Theme.of(context).colorScheme.inversePrimary,

        title: Text(title),
      ),
      body: Center(
        child: Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
          children: <Widget>[
            SelectionArea(
              child: Column(
                crossAxisAlignment: CrossAxisAlignment.start,
                children: [
                  Text('Broken'),
                  ...List.generate(
                    5,
                    (index) => Text.rich(
                      TextSpan(
                        children: [
                          WidgetSpan(
                            alignment: PlaceholderAlignment.middle,
                            child: Text('$index. '),
                          ),
                          TextSpan(
                            text:
                                "This is a long line like you'd see in a bulleted list",
                          ),
                        ],
                      ),
                    ),
                  ),
                  SizedBox(height: 16),
                  Text('Functional'),
                  ...List.generate(
                    5,
                    (index) => Text.rich(
                      TextSpan(
                        children: [
                          TextSpan(text: '$index. '),
                          TextSpan(
                            text:
                                "This is a long line like you'd see in a bulleted list",
                          ),
                        ],
                      ),
                    ),
                  ),
                ],
              ),
            ),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

```
</details>

From my initial investigation forward selection seems to work fine, but backwards selection is broken.

Take the following simplified example with a two bulleted list entries. A bullet list entry is broken into two selectables, the bullet followed by the text in the entry. So, a1 is the bullet, and a2 is the text for the bullet list entry, and similar for the b selectables.

[[a1, a2], [b1, b2]]

Now let's start our selection by clicking on b2, the text from the second bullet list entry.

The issue here is that the selection is found in selectable b2, select backwards moving pointer to selectable a1, selection moves backwards to b1 and then out of selection container b and into selection container a which holds a1 and a2. We start to look for the selection starting with selectable a1, the selection search stops in a1 since the pointer is there, with no reason to search for selection in a2. The key issue here is that when we moved the selection backwards and entered a new selection container our search began at the beginning of the container, rather than at the end which would logically follow the beginning of the next container when moving backwards.

Why does forward selection work? Selection starts in a1, select forward moving the pointer to selectable b1, selection moves to forward to selectable a2 and then into selection container b which holds b1 and b2, starting to find selection starting with selectable b1, the selection search stops in b1 since the pointer is there. Forward selection works because the selection follows the logical order of selectables, where backward selection it does not. 

A possible fix here is for `MultiSelectableSelectionContainerDelegate._initSelection` and `MultiSelectableSelectionContainerDelegate._adjustSelection` to have more accurate backward/forward detection. When moving the selection backwards and reaching a new selection container, maybe we should start looking for the selection in the last selectable in that container rather than the first.

I'm not actively working on this but hope this information is helpful. Also happy to review any pull requests fixing this.

issues.title: Tab+Shift behaviour is not consistent with other web applications
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill out the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

This is a very specific issue that might indicate a broad set of differences in key listening between Flutter and other applications.

If you have a list of focusable widgets and press Tab+Shift (in this specific order) then focus will jump to the next widget and will get back to the previous one.

That doesn't match any other web application. If you use the Tab+Shift combination there, the focus will get to the next input and stay there. Basically, this combination will be treated as a single Tab key.

See the videos below for examples.

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- You must include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Paste the following code:
```
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      debugShowCheckedModeBanner: false,
      home: Scaffold(
        body: Center(
          child: MyWidget(),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

class MyWidget extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Padding(
      padding: const EdgeInsets.all(16),
      child: Column(
        children: [
          TextField(),
          TextField(),
          TextField(),
          TextField(),
          TextField(),
        ],
      ),
    );
  }
}

```
2. Launch web application
3. Focus the second text field
4. Press Tab+Shift combination
5. Focus jumps and stays at the same place

<details>
  <summary>Expected behaviour (non-Flutter application):</summary>

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13119003/112472624-6b44d180-8d7e-11eb-8fe5-b67b5fde3a6a.mp4


</details>

issues.title: [Android] CJK IME candidate window position error with external keyboard on Android
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Connect a physical keyboard (e.g., a Bluetooth keyboard) to your Android device.
2. Switch to a CJK input method (e.g., Chinese) and start typing to trigger candidate words.

### Expected results

The candidate window should be displayed correctly near the cursor to ensure a seamless user experience. With the increasing popularity of Android tablets, many of which are commonly used with physical keyboards, it has become essential to address this issue. Correctly positioning the candidate window is critical for maintaining usability and consistency across devices, especially for users relying on CJK input methods. Fixing this bug will significantly improve the input experience for a growing segment of Android users.

### Actual results

On Android devices, the candidate window for the input method is incorrectly displayed in the lower-left corner of the screen. In contrast, on iOS and desktop devices, the candidate window appears in the correct position. I used Gboard as the input method on Android and tested other input methods as well, but the issue persisted. This suggests that the problem is not related to a specific input method.

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>
This is a very simple example that contains only a textfiled.

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        body: Center(
          child: TextField(),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

</details>

I use a vivo iqoo mobile phone and a Xiaomi tablet, and have adopted the open-source fcitx input method and Google's Gboard. However, no matter which input method is used, its floating window cannot be displayed correctly. And I have conducted experiments in different Flutter applications, all of which cannot be displayed correctly. It seems that this is not a problem with the input method, but a problem with the Flutter framework. Most Android tablets sold on the market are equipped with Bluetooth keyboards, so the coverage of this kind of problem is extremely wide. An effective solution is urgently needed. I sincerely request the official to pay attention to it.

issues.title: Selection of any justified-text is inaccurate in non-latin languages
issues.body:
I hope I can clarify this issue because it is a little weird. It took me a while to figure out when exactly does it occur.

Here is a very primitive application:

```dart
// main.dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
void main() => runApp(MaterialApp(
  home: Scaffold(
    appBar: AppBar(),
    body: Padding(
      padding: const EdgeInsets.all(18.0),
      child: TextField(
        textAlign: TextAlign.justify,
        maxLines: null,
      ),
    ),
  ),
));
```

Now, type any latin charachter text. Try to select any single or multiple words, you will find that the selection blue highlight matches accurately the boundaries of the word(s). See this screen shot:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3346502/64191394-5a3e2200-ceb3-11e9-82a9-6f48d6333256.png)

_____________________

Then, put some Korean text. You will find that some words are poorly highlighted:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3346502/64191512-9bcecd00-ceb3-11e9-85ea-4002d85f54da.png)

> 한국어(韓國語)는 대한민국과 조선민주주의인민공화국의 공용어로, 대한민국에서는 한국어 또는 한국말이라고 부르고, 조선민주주의인민공화국에서는 조선말이라고 부른다.

_____________________

Also in Arabic texts (it does not matter here that the `textDirection` is not RTL. Both RTL and LTR have the same issue):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3346502/64191648-e18b9580-ceb3-11e9-8e5c-6fe5a3bb1dba.png)

> العربية لغة رسمية في كل دول الوطن العربي إضافة إلى كونها لغة رسمية في تشاد وإريتريا وإسرائيل. وهي إحدى اللغات الرسمية الست في منظمة الأمم المتحدة، ويُحتفل باليوم العالمي للغة العربية في 18 ديسمبر كذكرى اعتماد العربية بين لغات العمل في الأمم المتحدة.

_____________________

Same in Hebrew:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3346502/64191829-5068ee80-ceb4-11e9-84f2-780841b134b3.png)

> עִבְרִית היא שפה שמית, ממשפחת השפות האפרו-אסיאתיות, הידועה כשפתם של היהודים ושל השומרונים, אשר ניב מודרני שלה (עברית ישראלית) הוא שפתה הרשמית של מדינת ישראל, מעמד שעוגן בשנת 2018 בחוק יסוד: ישראל – מדינת הלאום של העם היהודי.

_____________________

Just copy these texts and paste them in the input field, then try to select one word. In all cases above I just long tapped on one word and the highlight missed the word boundry as shown.

It does have to be a text field or input though. Any justified selectable text would reproduce the same behavior. I tried in both stable and master channels.

Here is my `flutter doctor` output:

```
Doctor summary (to see all details, run flutter doctor -v):
[✓] Flutter (Channel master, v1.9.8-pre.54, on Linux, locale en_US.UTF-8)
 
[✓] Android toolchain - develop for Android devices (Android SDK version 29.0.2)
[✓] Android Studio (version 3.5)
[✓] VS Code (version 1.37.1)
[✓] Connected device (1 available)

• No issues found!
```
comments: CC @GaryQian

It does not have to be a text input to reproduce the issue. Any selectable text would show the same behavior (text inputs, SelectableText, SelectableText.rich ... etc). So I think it's better to remove `a: text input` label.

@OsamaAbbas The labels are not exclusive, if it impacts an area or is reasonably relevant, we should add the label so that the right people can find the right bug.

This looks like we are somehow missing the justification_x_offset from the code_units in LibTxt. The curious part is somehow, alphabetic text works, which indicates it isn't a blatant omission of the justification_x_offset but rather something more subtle.
Russian language does NOT have this issue. (I thought it would, because it uses non latin chars)

A more likely explanation is that some scripts treat spaces as forming a new run of text, while latin script treats spaces as a part of the same run of text. I'm reviewing the justify logic to see how we deal with spaces as a delimiter for runs and if we are justifying properly there.

I really appreciate your effort. Thank you.

Maybe related: Mixed text (latin and non latin characters) breaks the RTL justification but fixes the selection accuracy! However, not all combinations have this effect, some combinations only fixes the selection accuracy on the same line. I did many trials but have not recorded them. By a 'combination' I mean: single latin character, one character plus a space, one word without spaces, multi-words ... etc.

The handles of the selection highlight (in non latin text) is also inaccurate in the other `textAlign` cases in the case of multi word selection.

Third problem: the spaces between words are given a smaller height than the words.

![20190909_151634](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3346502/64507214-0ef19c80-d315-11e9-9554-7b398dc5745f.jpg)

An important update: changing the font to an appropriate Arabic font (I used Amiri Font) solves the accuracy problem. It also makes spaces have the same height as the surrounding text.

I will try with a Korean font later.

You can now use `selectionHeightStyle` and `selectionWidthStyle` to choose how the selection highlighting will look. See https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/48917

I recommend trying `max` or `strut`
Actually, the issue above only partially resolves this. Reopening!

Any updates on this issue? [This](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/39755#issuecomment-529315991) is something we're facing too in Arabic text selection no matter what the text alignment is. It's not just a justification issue, and I think the title should reflect that. We've tried different fonts and no solution. Even on latest release v1.17.1. Thank you.

incorrect selection in  arabic (RTL) language

### Update: After investigating a bit more into this issue I can confirm that it has to do something with the font being used. In another application with a custom font containing glyphs for Persian language it's working as intended but using the default font brings back the issue. ![Nov-01-2021 13-35-49](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8766492/139655432-5a48c810-52d9-446e-b5a1-70a31d3295a2.gif) Top textfield uses a custom font and bottom textfield is using default font (I guess Roboto because of `MaterialApp`). --- 2021 is about to end and it's been open since 2019. I'm willing to start working on it but I'm not familiar with Flutter's huge codebase and this issue seems to be related to many places from Flutter's engine to the Flutter's text related widgets.
